text:
Background: Medical image segmentation is a foundational step in image processing, and the ability to accurately compare segmented images is vital for measuring research progress. However, evaluating medical segmentation presents several significant challenges: the difficulty of selecting appropriate metrics from a diverse pool; the prevalence of inconsistent metric definitions in existing literature; the computational inefficiency of many implementations when processing large 3D volumes; and a general lack of support for fuzzy (probabilistic) segmentations within standard evaluation frameworks.

Results: We provide a comprehensive overview of 20 evaluation metrics selected through an extensive literature review. To support fuzzy segmentation—which characterizes the membership level of voxels across multiple classes—we provide fuzzy mathematical definitions for all 20 metrics. We also analyze the specific properties of these metrics to serve as a practical guide for researchers in selecting the most appropriate evaluation tools for their specific tasks. Finally, we introduce an advanced, open-source evaluation tool that implements these metrics using highly optimized algorithms. This tool is designed to maintain high performance in terms of speed and memory efficiency even when processing massive datasets, such as whole-body MRI or CT volumes.

Conclusion: This work provides both a high-performance evaluation tool for 3D medical image segmentation and a set of formal guidelines to help researchers choose a statistically sound subset of metrics tailored to their data and clinical objectives.
